coredata.dc:title: Engineering heterogeneous Z-scheme photocatalysts Fe-BTC/CuInS2/BiVO4 integrated with carbon quantum dots to enhance the efficiency of Cr(VI) reduction and RR-195 degradation under visible light

coredata.dc:description: In this study, Fe-BTC/CuInS2/BiVO4 composites (with Fe-BTC content ranging from 10 to 30 wt%) were synthesized followed by the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to form FCB-CQD photocatalysts. The resulting materials exhibit several key advantages, including high surface area, large pore volume, small particle size, efficient visible light absorption, and improved charge transport properties. The integration of CQDs significantly enhanced charge separation and reduced electron–hole recombination. A Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism was proposed and validated, enabling the FCB-CQD photocatalysts to achieve highly efficient photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and degradation of Reactive Red 195 (RR-195) under visible light. The 20%FCB-CQD sample achieved removal efficiencies exceeding 98 % for both pollutants. Key operational parameters, such as catalyst dosage, pollutant concentration, pH, reactive species involvement, and photocatalyst stability, were systematically investigated. Electrochemical and optical characterizations were used to construct the band structure and elucidate the electron transfer pathway. Scavenger experiments confirmed the dominant active species involved in each process, providing insight into the underlying photocatalytic mechanisms. These findings demonstrate the promising potential of FCB-CQD as an efficient and reusable photocatalyst for environmental remediation applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, Fe-BTC/CuInS2/BiVO4 composites (with Fe-BTC content ranging from 10 to 30 wt%) were synthesized followed by the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to form FCB-CQD photocatalysts. The resulting materials exhibit several key advantages, including high surface area, large pore volume, small particle size, efficient visible light absorption, and improved charge transport properties. The integration of CQDs significantly enhanced charge separation and reduced electron–hole recombination. A Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism was proposed and validated, enabling the FCB-CQD photocatalysts to achieve highly efficient photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and degradation of Reactive Red 195 (RR-195) under visible light. The 20%FCB-CQD sample achieved removal efficiencies exceeding 98 % for both pollutants. Key operational parameters, such as catalyst dosage, pollutant concentration, pH, reactive species involvement, and photocatalyst stability, were systematically investigated. Electrochemical and optical characterizations were used to construct the band structure and elucidate the electron transfer pathway. Scavenger experiments confirmed the dominant active species involved in each process, providing insight into the underlying photocatalytic mechanisms. These findings demonstrate the promising potential of FCB-CQD as an efficient and reusable photocatalyst for environmental remediation applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution caused by heavy metals, synthetic dyes, and persistent organic pollutants poses a significant threat to human health and aquatic ecosystems [1–3]. Among various contaminants, hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] and reactive azo dyes such as Reactive Red 195 (RR-195) are of particular concern due to their high toxicity, stability, and resistance to biodegradation. Cr(VI), commonly released from electroplating, tanning, and pigment industries [4–6], is highly soluble and carcinogenic [7–9], while RR-195, a widely used textile dye, is difficult to degrade and can cause mutagenic and cytotoxic effects even at low concentrations [10]. Various technologies such as adsorption, ion exchange, membrane filtration, and chemical precipitation have been explored for the remediation of these pollutants [11–14]. However, many of these methods are costly, energy-intensive, or generate secondary waste. In contrast, photocatalysis has emerged as a green and effective approach, capable of converting hazardous compounds into less toxic products under light irradiation [15]. The key to achieving high photocatalytic efficiency lies in designing multifunctional semiconductors that can effectively absorb visible light, promote charge separation, and generate reactive species.

Among various semiconductors, CuInS2 has garnered attention due to its narrow bandgap (∼1.5–1.6 eV), strong visible-light absorption, and suitable conduction band position for reducing Cr(VI) [16,17]. However, its photocatalytic activity is often limited by rapid electron-hole recombination and low surface area [8–10]. To mitigate these limitations, coupling CuInS2 with a wide-bandgap semiconductor such as BiVO4 has been explored to construct heterojunction systems that promote charge separation and enhance overall activity. For instance, CuInS2/BiVO4 composites have been successfully applied under visible light to degrade organic dyes such as methylene blue, rhodamine B, and methyl orange, as well as to inactivate Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacteria [18]. BiVO4 contributes oxidative functionality due to its favourable valence band position and exhibits good photochemical stability, making it suitable for the degradation of recalcitrant organics. Nevertheless, BiVO4 is constrained by its inherently low charge carrier mobility and susceptibility to rapid recombination of photoinduced charges. To further enhance charge separation and increase the active surface area, we incorporated Fe-BTC, an iron-based metal–organic framework (MOF), into the composite for the first time. Fe-BTC provides a large specific surface area, tunable porosity, and abundant adsorption sites, which are favorable for reactant diffusion and intermediate adsorption [19]. In addition, the presence of Fe3+/Fe2+ redox pairs in Fe-BTC can participate in electron transfer, contributing to the photocatalytic redox process [10]. While combining semiconductors into heterojunctions can improve photocatalytic efficiency, the interface compatibility and charge transfer between components remain a challenge. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as effective electron mediators due to their excellent conductivity, visible-light absorption, and ability to form intimate interfaces between semiconductors [20]. CQDs can act as electron bridges to facilitate Z-scheme charge transfer, which preserves the strong redox potential of the individual semiconductors while improving charge separation [21].

In this study, we report the rational design and synthesis of a novel Z-scheme photocatalyst system consisting of Fe-BTC, CuInS2, and BiVO4 integrated with CQDs (denoted as FCB-CQD) via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The integration aims to (i) exploit the strong reduction capability of CuInS2, (ii) harness the oxidative potential of BiVO4, (iii) utilise the high surface area and porosity of Fe-BTC, and (iv) enhance interfacial charge transfer through CQD mediation. The photocatalytic performance of FCB-CQD was evaluated for the simultaneous reduction of Cr(VI) and degradation of RR-195 dye under visible-light irradiation. A comprehensive investigation of the structure–activity relationship, active species participation, and photocatalytic mechanism was conducted to elucidate the synergistic interactions among the components and the crucial role of CQDs in facilitating Z-scheme charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CuInS2 and Fe-BTC materials
ce:section.ce:para: CuInS2 was synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Specifically, 10 mmol of sodium dodecyl sulfate (CH3(CH2)11SO4Na) was dissolved in 50 mL of ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) and stirred vigorously at 70 °C for 15 min to form Solution A. Simultaneously, 2 mmol of indium chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O), 2 mmol of opper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), and 5 mmol of thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2) were dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water to form Solution B. Solution A was then slowly added to Solution B under continuous stirring at 500 rpm for 30 min at 70 °C. The resulting mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, a black adhesive precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed multiple times with distilled water, and dried at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain the CuInS2 photocatalyst.

The Fe-BTC material was synthesized using a hydrothermal method assisted by microwave irradiation. Specifically, 12 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O was dissolved in 60 mL of deionised water, while 12 mmol of trimesic acid (H3BTC) was dissolved in 60 mL of 1 M NaOH solution. The H3BTC solution was sonicated for 30 min to ensure complete dissolution and homogeneity. The two solutions were then combined and stirred for an additional 30 min to form a uniform mixture, which was subsequently transferred into a Teflon-lined vessel. The mixture was subjected to microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment at 100 °C for 30 min, resulting in the formation of an orange suspension. The resulting solid product was collected by centrifugation, thoroughly washed with deionised water and ethanol to remove any unreacted precursors, and then dried at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain the Fe-BTC material.

The Fe-BTC/CuInS2/BiVO4/CQD materials was also synthesized using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Initially, 20 mL of CQD solution was diluted in a mixed solvent of deionised water and ethanol at a volume ratio of 4:1. Next, 0.2 g of Fe-BTC, 0.7 g of CuInS2, and 0.1 g of BiVO4 were added to the CQD solution. The suspension was then sonicated for 1 h to ensure uniform dispersion. The resulting mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined reactor and subjected to microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment at 100 °C for 30 min. After natural cooling to room temperature (∼30 °C), the black precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed thoroughly with deionised water, and dried at 100 °C for 8 h to obtain the final 20%FCB-CQD photocatalyst.

Following this procedure, composites with varying Fe-BTC content, 10%FCB-CQD and 30%FCB-CQD, were also prepared using the same method. The corresponding synthesis scheme is illustrated in Fig. S1, and the detailed composition of each formulation is provided in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photocatalytic activity for Cr(VI) reduction and RR-195 degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic performance of CuInS2, BiVO4, Fe-BTC, FCB, and FCB-CQD photocatalysts was evaluated through the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and the degradation of the dye RR-195. In a typical experiment, 100 mL of an aqueous solution containing either Cr(VI) (20 mg/L) or RR-195 (50 mg/L) was mixed with the photocatalyst at a loading of 0.3 g/L. The suspension was stirred in the dark for 60 min to establish adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Following this, the suspension was irradiated using a 300 W Xenon lamp as the visible light source to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. At predetermined time intervals, 2 mL aliquots of the reaction mixture were withdrawn, and the solid photocatalyst was separated by centrifugation at 6000 rpm. The residual concentrations of Cr(VI) and RR-195 in the supernatant were measured using UV–Vis spectroscopy at their characteristic wavelengths of 350 or 540 nm, respectively [22–24]. The photocatalytic efficiency (%H) for either reduction or degradation was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
% H =

)

where C o, C t are the initial and time-dependent concentrations of Cr(VI) or RR-195, respectively.

To investigate operational parameters, the effects of catalyst dosage (0.1–0.5 g/L), initial pollutant concentration (10–50 mg/L for Cr(VI); 10–70 mg/L for RR-195), and solution pH (3, 5, 6, 8, and 10) were systematically studied.

The role of reactive species in the photocatalytic mechanism was further examined through radical trapping experiments. Specific scavengers were added to the reaction system, including ammonium oxalate monohydrate (AOM), silver nitrate (AgNO3), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), tert-butanol (TBA) to capture holes (h+), electrons (e–), superoxide radicals (•O2 –), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH), respectively. The recyclability of the 20%FCB-CQD photocatalyst was evaluated over five consecutive reaction cycles, and its physicochemical properties after photocatalysis were examined to assess structural stability and performance retention.

The crystalline structure of Fe-BTC, BiVO4, CuInS2, and FCB-CQD samples was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD pattern of BiVO4 exhibits diffraction peaks at 2θ of 18.78°, 28.72°, 30.48°, 34.73°, 39.70°, 42.18°, 45.72°, and 46.92°, which correspond to the monoclinic phase of BiVO4 (JCPDS No. 14–0688) [20]. The CuInS2 sample, synthesized via hydrothermal treatment, shows characteristic peaks at 2θ of 27.9°, 32.34°, and 46.2°, corresponding to the (112), (004), and (204) planes of the chalcopyrite CuInS2 phase (JCPDS: No. 27–0159) [25–27]. The Fe-BTC sample exhibits distinct peaks at 2θ of 10.52°, 10.99°, 14.68°, 16.45°, 19.1°, 20.14°, 24.15°, and 27.75°, consistent with previously reported Fe-BTC structures [10].

The XRD patterns of the FCB-CQD composites contain all the major peaks associated with Fe-BTC, CuInS2, and BiVO4, confirming the successful incorporation of each component (Figure 1). However, the characteristic peaks of the BiVO4 and Fe-BTC are significantly weakened or absent in some cases due to their lower content and high dispersion within the composite matrix. Notably, the intensity of the Fe-BTC peak at 2θ = 10.99° increases proportionally with Fe-BTC loading (from 10 to 30 wt%), while the intensity of the CuInS2 peaks decreases. These observations confirm the successful synthesis of FCB-CQD materials via the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method and indicate compositional variation across different formulations.

The elemental composition and chemical states of Fe-BTC, BiVO4, CuInS2, and FCB-CQD materials were analyzed using X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS). The wide-scan XPS spectra confirmed the presence of key elements, including C 1s (284 eV), N 1s (400 eV), O 1s (532 eV), S 2p (161 eV), Bi 4f (159 eV), V 4f (517 eV), Cu 2p (932 eV) and In 3d (445 eV) in all samples (Fig. S2) [19,28,29]. Quantitative XPS analysis (Table S3) showed that BiVO4 consists primarily of O (66.03 mol%), Bi (17.18 mol%), and V (16.79 mol%), while CuInS2 is composed of Cu (23.77 mol%), In (24.40 mol%), and S (51.83 mol%). The 20 %FCB-CQD composite includes all expected elements, including C, O, S, Fe, In, Cu, Bi, V, and N, further confirming the successful integration of CQDs with the Fe-BTC/CuInS2/BiVO4 system.

High-resolution XPS spectra revealed detailed chemical environments of each component (Fig. 2 ). For Fe-BTC, peaks corresponding to C=C/C–C (284.60 eV), C–O (286.09 eV), C=O (288.51 eV), O=C–O (290.10 eV) were observed in the C 1s region. The O 1s spectrum displayed contributions from Fe–O (529.92 eV), chemisorbed oxygen (531.50 eV), and carboxylate oxygen (532.61 eV). The Fe 2p spectrum indicated the coexistence of Fe2+ (711.28 and 724.77 eV) and Fe3+ (713.79 and 727.24 eV) oxidation states [19]. The XPS profile of CuInS2 showed Cu+ (931.86 and 951.63 eV), Cu2+ (934.27 and 954.31 eV), In2+ (445.00 and 452.55 eV), In3+ (446.04 and 453.62 eV), and S2– (161.75 and 162.93 eV) [28]. For BiVO4, the Bi 4f region exhibited peaks at 159.10 and 164.39 eV for Bi3+, while the V 2p region showed V5+ peaks at 516.30 and 523.51 eV. The O 1s spectrum included lattice oxygen (529.98 eV) and surface-adsorbed oxygen (531.62 eV) [30].

In the 20%FCB-CQD composite, all characteristic elements of Fe-BTC, CuInS2, and BiVO4 were retained. The binding energies for Fe2+ (711.42 and 724.80 eV), Fe3+ (714.02 and 727.27 eV), Cu+ (931.71 and 951.53 eV), Cu2+ (933.07 and 953.22 eV), In2+ (444.67 and 452.28 eV), In3+ (445.91 and 453.44 eV), Bi3+ (159.50 and 164.81 eV), V5+ (516.46 and 523.72 eV) and S2– (161.51 and 162.85 eV) were consistent with those of the individual components. The C 1s and O 1s spectra showed typical peaks for C=C/C–C (284.75 eV), C–O (286.24 eV), C=O (288.61 eV), O=C–O (290.67 eV), Fe–O (530.65 eV), chemisorbed oxygen (531.72 eV), and O–C=O (532.93 eV). Notably, the binding energies of C 1s, O 1s, Fe 2p, Bi 4f, and V 2p exhibited a positive shift, indicating a decrease in electron density in the Fe-BTC and BiVO4 phases, likely due to interfacial charge redistribution upon CQD integration [31].

In contrast, the Cu 2p, In 3d, and S 2p binding energies showed a negative shift compared to those in pristine CuInS2, indicating an increase in electron density in the CuInS2 phase. These spectral changes collectively suggest that electrons are transferred from Fe-BTC and BiVO4 phases to CuInS2, with CQDs acting as efficient mediators to facilitate this Z-Scheme charge transfer pathway.

The elemental composition of FCB-CQD was further analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in Fig. S3, Fe content increases proportionally with the Fe-BTC loading from 10 to 30 wt% in the FCB-CQD composites. Fig. 3 presents EDS elemental mapping for C, O, S, Fe, In, Cu, Bi, V, and N, revealing a uniform elemental distribution, indicating good dispersion within the composite structure. Together, the XRD, XPS, and EDS analyzes confirm the successful synthesis and integration of Fe-BTC, CuInS2, and BiVO4 with carbon quantum dots in the FCB-CQD photocatalysts.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of CuInS2, BiVO4, Fe-BTC, and FCB-CQD materials are presented in Fig. 4 . According to IUPAC classification [32], CuInS2 and BiVO4 exhibit type III isotherms, indicative of weak adsorbate–adsorbent interactions, while Fe-BTC shows a type I isotherm, characteristic of microporous materials.

As summarised in Table 1 , CuInS2 and BiVO4 possess low surface areas (13.22 and 11.27 m2/g, respectively) and limited pore volumes (0.07 and 0.052 cm3/g), which may hinder reactant diffusion. In contrast, Fe-BTC exhibits a significantly larger surface area (1218.50 m2/g) and pore volume (0.829 cm3/g), promoting enhanced reactant accessibility. Upon integration with Fe-BTC, the FCB-CQD composites demonstrate a marked improvement in textural properties. As Fe-BTC content increases from 10 to 30 wt%, the surface area rises from 114.28 to 301.31 m2/g and pore volume from 0.147 to 0.343 cm3/g, while the average pore diameter decreases from 5.53 to 3.48 nm, consistent with the smaller pore size of Fe-BTC. These enhancements suggest that FCB-CQD materials possess improved adsorption and diffusion characteristics, contributing to more efficient photocatalytic performance.

The morphology of Fe-BTC, CuInS2, BiVO4, and FBC-CQD materials was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 5 A, BiVO4 and Fe-BTC exhibit relatively uniform spherical morphologies with particle sizes ranging from 10 to 20 and 20–50 nm, respectively. The CuInS2 material shows a distinctive flower-like structure with large particle sizes of 300–500 nm, distributed evenly across the sample. TEM images of the FBC-CQD materials reveal that the flower-like morphology of CuInS2 is retained. Additionally, uniform nanoparticles (∼10–20 nm), corresponding to Fe-BTC and BiVO4, are clearly observed surrounding the CuInS2 structure. This confirms the successful integration of Fe-BTC, CuInS2, and BiVO4 into the composite matrix.

The optical and electrochemical properties of CuInS2, Fe-BTC, BiVO4, and FCB-CQD were investigated using UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS), photoluminescence (PL), Mott-Schottky, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to assess light absorption capability, band-gap energy, band positions, charge carrier recombination, and charge transfer efficiency (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6A, the UV–Vis DRS spectrum of CuInS2 demonstrates strong visible-light absorption. The band-gap energies of Fe-BTC, BiVO4, CuInS2, 10%FCB-CQD, 20%FCB-CQD, and 30%FCB-CQD materials are measured to be 2.68, 2.37, 1.6, 1.72, 1.83, and 1.96 eV, respectively (Fig. 6B), demonstrating tunable optical properties through composite formation.

As shown in Fig. 6C, CuInS2 exhibits a high PL intensity, indicating rapid electron-hole recombination. Upon integration with Fe-BTC and CQD, the PL intensity is significantly reduced, suggesting suppressed recombination. This improvement is attributed to more efficient charge separation and transport, likely facilitated by CQDs, which serve as conductive bridges enabling rapid electron migration and reducing recombination losses. Additionally, the increased surface area of FCB-CQD further contributes to reduced recombination, as the larger pore network provides more space for charge carrier migration (Fig. 6D). The trend in PL intensity, in the order of Fe-BTC > 10%FCB-CQD > 20%FCB-CQD > 30%FCB-CQD (Fig. 6C), further supports the role of this MOF to enhance charge separation in the composite systems. Notably, EIS reveals that the charge transfer resistance decreases with increasing Fe-BTC content, confirming improved electrical conductivity and interfacial charge transport (Fig. 6D). This is likely due to the CQDs acting as an interfacial binder, enhancing contact between semiconductors and facilitating efficient charge flow [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of CuInS2, BiVO4, Fe-BTC and FCB-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of CuInS2, BiVO4, Fe-BTC, and FCB-CQD materials was evaluated based on their ability to reduce Cr(VI) and degrade RR-195 dye under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S4, the adsorption efficiencies of Cr(VI) after 60 min in the dark were 12.48 % for CuInS2, 9.52 % for BiVO4, 15.39 % for Fe-BTC, and 13.22 % for 20%FCB-CQD, indicating moderate adsorption capacity across all materials. Following 90 min of visible light exposure (Fig. 7 A), the photocatalytic Cr(VI) removal efficiencies increased substantially, reaching 50.54 % for BiVO4, 60.55 % for Fe-BTC, 67.03 % for CuInS2, 83.12 % for 20%FCB, and 96.05 % for 20%FCB-CQD. A similar trend was observed in the degradation of RR-195 dye after 60 min of irradiation, with efficiencies of 53.26 , 68.59 , 74.18 , 86.45 , and 98.63 % for BiVO4, CuInS2, Fe-BTC, 20%FCB, and 20%FCB-CQD, respectively (Fig. 8 A). Among the FCB-CQD composites, 20 %FCB-CQD achieved the highest performance, with 96.05 % Cr(VI) reduction and 98.63 % RR-195 degradation, compared to 92.31 % and 92.64 % for 10 %FCB-CQD, and 94.08 % and 96.02 % for 30%FCB-CQD, respectively. The slightly lower efficiency of 30 %FCB-CQD is likely due to its increased bandgap energy (Fig. 6B), which limits visible-light absorption. The enhanced activity of 20%FCB-CQD is attributed to the synergistic effect of the integrated components, including Fe-BTC, CuInS 2 , BiVO 4 , and CQDs, which together provide a higher surface area, improved light harvesting, reduced electron–hole recombination, and more efficient charge transport. In particular, the incorporation of CQDs significantly boosts charge mobility by acting as conductive bridges, as confirmed by EIS (Fig. 6D). Given its superior photocatalytic efficiency, the 20%FCB-CQD sample was selected for further investigation into key operational parameters affecting photocatalysis, including catalyst dosage, pollutant concentration (Cr(VI) and RR-195), pH, active species involvement, and material stability.

The effect of the 20 %FCB-CQD photocatalyst dosage on Cr(VI) efficiency is presented in Fig. 7B. As the dosage increases from 0.1 to 0.4 g/L, Cr(VI) reduction efficiency rises from 66.13 % to 98.12 %. However, a further increase to 0.5 g/L results in a slight decrease in efficiency, likely due to excessive catalyst loading hindering light penetration and reducing electron–hole separation efficiency [33]. A similar trend is observed in the RR-195 degradation results (Fig. 8B), with efficiencies of 58.24 %, 82.98 %, 98.63 %, 99.22 %, and 95.46 % corresponding to catalyst dosages of 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 g/L, respectively. These results indicate that 0.4 g/L is the optimal dosage for achieving maximum photocatalytic performance in both Cr(VI) and RR-195 removal. Therefore, this dosage was selected for subsequent experiments investigating the effects of pH and pollutant concentration.

The pH of the reaction solution significantly influences the photocatalytic efficiency of Cr(VI) reduction and RR-195 degradation by affecting the surface charge of the catalyst and its interaction with target pollutants [34–36]. As shown in Fig. 7C, the Cr(VI) removal efficiency using 20 %FCB-CQD reaches 99.25 %, 98.12 %, 90.62 %, 75.05 %, and 50.36 % at pH values of 3, 5, 6, 8, and 10, respectively. Under acidic conditions, the abundance of H+ ions facilitates the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III), as described by Equation (2) [16,37]. In contrast, at higher pH, Cr(OH)3 forms and deposits on the catalyst surface (Equation (3), which not only blocks active sites [38–40] but also increases electron–hole recombination, thereby reducing photocatalytic efficiency [41].

A similar pH-dependent trend is observed for RR-195 degradation (Fig. 8C), where the removal efficiencies are 99.45 %, 99.22 %, 89.82 %, 83.28 %, and 66.71 % at pH 3, 5, 6, 8, and 10, respectively. At pH < 7, the positively charged surface of the 20 %FCB-CQD catalyst promotes electrostatic attraction with the negatively charged RR-195 dye, enhancing degradation efficiency. When pH exceeds 7, the catalyst surface becomes negatively charged, reducing electrostatic interaction and thus lowering the degradation performance [42]. Although the highest efficiencies are achieved at pH 3, the study adopts pH 5 for further experiments to balance high performance with practical applicability, and to assess the effects of pollutant concentration on photocatalytic activity.

The initial concentration of Cr(VI) plays a critical role in the photocatalytic efficiency of the 20%FCB-CQD material. As shown in Fig. 7D, Cr(VI) removal efficiency decreases with increasing initial concentration. Specifically, at concentrations of 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mg/L, the Cr(VI) reduction efficiencies were 98.90 %, 98.45 %, 98.12 %, 90.53 %, 80.36 %, and 72.35 %, respectively. A similar trend was observed for RR-195 degradation (Fig. 8D), with efficiencies of 99.34 %, 98.88 %, 95.97 %, 85.28 %, and 72.31 % corresponding to initial dye concentrations of 10, 30, 50, 70, and 90 mg/L, respectively. The decline in efficiency at higher concentrations is attributed to the increased pollutant load, which requires more active sites and longer reaction times for effective treatment.

Under optimal conditions (a catalyst dosage of 0.4 g/L, Cr(VI) concentration of 20 mg/L, pH 5, 300 W Xenon lamp irradiation, and a reaction temperature of 25 °C), the 20 %FCB-CQD photocatalyst achieved 98.12 % Cr(VI) removal within 90 min. As summarized in Table 2 , this performance surpasses that of previously reported photocatalysts, including MIL-125(Ti)/ZnIn2S4, ZnO/ZIF-8, Cu–TiO2/CuO, CuInS2, and 5 %Er–CuInS2, highlighting the superior photocatalytic efficiency of the 20%FCB-CQD material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation mechanism of Cr(VI) and RR-195 over 20%FCB-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: In photocatalytic systems, the generation and involvement of reactive species such as holes (h+), electrons, superoxide (•O2 –) radicals, and hydroxyl (•OH) radicals play a crucial role in determining degradation efficiency [11]. To elucidate the dominant active species in the 20%FCB-CQD system, radical trapping experiments were performed using AOM, AgNO3, p-BQ and TBA to capture h+, e–, •O2 –, and •OH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7E, the addition of AgNO3 significantly inhibited Cr(VI) reduction, reducing efficiency to 42.84 %, confirming that electrons are the primary active species in the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). Moderate inhibition by p-BQ suggests that superoxide radicals also play a role, while AOM and TBA had minimal effects, indicating lesser contributions from h+ and •OH. The relative importance of active species in Cr(VI) reduction follows the order: e– > •O2 – > h+ > •OH.

For RR-195 degradation, Fig. 8E shows that after 50 min of irradiation, the degradation efficiencies were 52.14 % (p-BQ), 72.34 % (AOM), 82.36 % (TBA), and 95.12 % (AgNO3). These results indicate that superoxide radicals are the dominant species, followed by h+, •OH, and e–. Thus, the reactive species responsible for RR-195 degradation follow the order: •O2 – > h+ > •OH > e–.

The Mott-Schottky method was used to determine the flat band potential (Efb) of BiVO4, CuInS2, and Fe-BTC materials using an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution (pH 7). The Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. S5) show positive slopes, confirming that all materials exhibit n-type semiconductor behavior. The measured flat band potentials versus Ag/AgCl were –0.14 eV for BiVO4, –1.35 eV for CuInS2, and –0.46 eV for Fe-BTC. These values were converted to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) scale using Equation (4):
(4)
E

+

+ 0.059 × p H
where EAg/AgCl is the working potential using Ag/AgCl (pH of 7) and Eo Ag/AgCl = 0.1976 eV at 25 °C

The flat band potentials relative to NHE of BiVO4, CuInS2, and Fe-BTC are + 0.47, –0.74, and + 0.15 eV, respectively. Assuming a conduction band lies ∼ 0.2 eV below Efb for n-type materials [50], the calculated conduction band (CB) edges are + 0.27 eV, –0.94 eV, and –0.05 eV for BiVO4, CuInS2, and Fe-BTC, respectively. Combined with their respective bandgap energies, the valence band (VB) positions are + 2.64 eV (BiVO4), +0.66 eV (CuInS2), and + 2.63 eV (Fe-BTC). Based on these values and the results of radical scavenging experiments, a Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism is proposed for the 20%FCB-CQD photocatalyst (Fig. 9 ). Upon visible light irradiation, the photocatalyst absorbs photons with energy ≥ 1.83 eV, promoting electron–hole pair generation (Equation (5):

Electrons in the CB of CuInS2 (–0.94 eV) can readily reduce adsorbed O2 to form superoxide radicals, since this potential is more negative than the •O2 –/O2 redox potential (–0.33 eV, Equation (6).

Because the CB levels of BiVO4 (+0.27 eV) and Fe-BTC (–0.05 eV) are insufficiently negative, their electrons cannot reduce O2 effectively [19]. However, CQDs, tightly integrated within the composite, act as electron mediators. Electrons from BiVO4 and Fe-BTC recombine with holes in CuInS2 through the CQDs (Equation (7), facilitating spatial charge separation:

Meanwhile, the VB levels of BiVO4 and Fe-BTC are sufficiently positive to oxidize H2O or surface –OH groups, generating •OH (Equation (8).

Finally, the reactants •O2 −, h+, •OH, and e− participate in the photocatalytic reaction to reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) or decompose harmful organic compounds into less toxic products such as CO2 and H2O (Eq. (9).

XPS analysis further supports this mechanism. After five photocatalytic cycles, high-resolution XPS spectra of the 20 %FCB-CQD material show positive binding energy shifts for key elements, indicating electron transfer and partial charge depletion during Cr(VI) reduction (Fig. 2). Additionally, the Cr 2p spectrum (Fig. S6) reveals only Cr3+ peaks at 577.45 eV and 587.14 eV, with no Cr(VI) signals detected, confirming complete reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III).

The reusability of the 20%FCB-CQD photocatalyst was assessed through five consecutive cycles of Cr(VI) reduction and RR-195 degradation under visible light irradiation (Fig. 7F and 8F). After five cycles, the photocatalytic efficiency for both pollutants remained above 95 %, demonstrating excellent stability and durability. Fig. S7 shows the XRD pattern of the 20% FCB-CQD material before and after five reaction cycles, revealing no significant changes, which indicates its recyclability and supports its classification as a green and sustainable material [51]. These results highlight the high chemical and structural stability of the 20%FCB-CQD photocatalyst during the simultaneous reduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of RR-195 dye.

In this study, a Z-scheme Fe-BTC/CuInS2/BiVO4/CQD photocatalyst was successfully synthesized via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The resulting 20%FCB-CQD composite exhibited a high surface area (301.31 m2/g), pore volume (0.343 cm3/g), a narrow bandgap (1.86 eV), and well-distributed particle sizes, with CuInS2 in the range of 200–300 nm and Fe-BTC/BiVO4 around 20–40 nm. The integration of CQDs effectively enhanced visible-light absorption, suppressed electron–hole recombination, and facilitated efficient charge separation and transfer.

Under optimal conditions (catalyst dose of 0.4 g/L, Cr(VI) concentration of 20 mg/L, pH 5, and 90 min of visible light irradiation), the 20%FCB-CQD catalyst achieved 98.12 % Cr(VI) reduction to Cr(III). Scavenger studies revealed the dominant active species involved in Cr(VI) reduction followed the order: e– > •O2 – > •OH > h+. Under the same conditions, the catalyst also degraded 98.8 % of RR-195 dye (30 mg/L) within 50 min, with the active species acting in the order: •O2 – > h+ > •OH > e–. The catalyst demonstrated excellent stability and reusability, maintaining over 95 % removal efficiency for both pollutants after five consecutive cycles. Mechanistic analysis supported a Z-Scheme charge transfer pathway, as confirmed by XPS analysis and radical trapping experiments. These findings highlight the 20%FCB-CQD composite as a highly efficient, stable, and scalable photocatalyst for the simultaneous removal of heavy metals and organic pollutants from water, offering promising potential for practical environmental remediation applications.
